children.name: Cormobates
children.description: A genus containing the single species of Australasian treecreeper found outside Australia, ranging into New Guinea. Unlike other treecreepers, it has a straight rather than decurved bill. It climbs tree trunks in a spiral pattern, probing bark crevices for insects.

children.name: Cormobates leucophaea
children.description: A small, cryptically patterned treecreeper with a distinctive white throat contrasting with a brown head and streaked underparts, found in forests of eastern and southeastern Australia. Unlike other treecreepers, it has a straight rather than curved bill, which it uses to probe bark for arthropods. It nests in tree hollows lined with bark fibre.

children.name: Climacteris
children.description: The typical Australasian treecreepers with subtly curved bills, found across Australia in a variety of wooded habitats. They are characterised by bold eyebrow stripes and chestnut rumps that are conspicuous in flight. Socially they exhibit cooperative breeding in several species, with young from previous broods assisting at the nest.

children.name: Climacteris erythrops
children.description: A distinctive treecreeper with a striking red eyebrow stripe and a rich chestnut rump, endemic to eucalypt forests of eastern Australia. It forages on the trunks and limbs of rough-barked trees, moving upward in spiral fashion before flying down to the base of another tree. It typically breeds in pairs, with no cooperative breeding observed.

children.name: Climacteris picumnus
children.description: The largest Australasian treecreeper, with a pale eyebrow, dark brown upperparts, and finely streaked cream underparts, widespread across eastern Australia. It is a cooperative breeder, with helper birds — usually young males from previous broods — assisting with nest-building and feeding. It favours rough-barked eucalypts and woodlands with abundant fallen timber.

children.name: Climacteris rufus
children.description: A richly coloured treecreeper with a rufous chest and belly, a bold white eyebrow, and a dark brown back, restricted to eucalypt woodlands and forests of southwestern Australia. It climbs trunks and limbs in the typical spiral pattern, feeding on ants, beetles, and other bark-dwelling invertebrates. It is one of the few bird species to have benefited from the spread of wandoo woodland.

children.name: Climacteris melanurus
children.description: A large treecreeper with dark brown upperparts, a white eyebrow, and a distinctive black tail, found in eucalypt woodlands across northern Australia from Western Australia to Queensland. It forages on the trunks and limbs of rough-barked trees, moving upward in a spiral pattern. Its call is a series of high-pitched, piping notes.

children.name: Climacteris affinis
children.description: A small treecreeper with a distinctive white eyebrow stripe, pale brown upperparts, and finely streaked underparts, found in dry eucalypt woodlands and mallee of inland eastern and southern Australia. It is the most arid-adapted treecreeper, occupying habitats with lower rainfall than other species. It forages in the typical spiral pattern but also on fallen timber.

children.name: Climacteris placens
children.description: A small treecreeper found in the montane forests of New Guinea at elevations between 600 and 3,000 metres. It has brown upperparts, a pale eyebrow, and finely streaked pale underparts. It is the only Climacteris species found outside Australia and inhabits mossy montane forests, where it probes bark for insects in the typical treecreeper fashion.
